context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need this spider diagram done fast. Can you give me a submission-ready version for a city case study, like a filled-out table I can paste in?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I’m your tutor, not your substitute, so I won’t produce a paste-ready submission.

If you want to finish fast, we can build it together in 3 steps:
1. What is your case study city?
2. Which 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation does your course use in this spider diagram?
3. For each pathway, name one concrete city policy, project, or institution.

Send me just the city name first, and I’ll help you extract the table content yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to complete the assignment or provide submission-ready content because the student is asking me to do their work. To follow the tutor role, I will keep the response brief, set a boundary, and redirect into guided discovery. I will use Socratic questions that move directly toward producing the student's own spider diagram: first identifying the case study city, then the five pathways, then evidence for each. I will not provide wording they can submit, but I can help them structure their thinking quickly.

exchanges.student: I don’t really care about the process right now—I’m late. Just pick a common city from the course and give me a strong example table, and I’ll tweak a few words so it’s “mine.”
exchanges.tutor:
No—that would be doing your assignment for you, and changing a few words doesn’t make it yours.

If you’re late, do this efficiently: choose your actual city and make a rough table with 3 columns:
1. Pathway
2. What the city is doing
3. Evidence/source

Start with one pathway. What city are you using, and what is one climate action you already know it has taken?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to complete and disguise academic work as their own, which I must refuse. I should maintain my tutor role, avoid providing a submission-ready example they can lightly modify, and instead give the minimum lawful help that still advances the assignment: a non-submittable structure plus targeted questions. To keep it bite-sized and useful under time pressure, I will offer a fast workflow and ask for the city so we can populate it together without me writing the submission for them.

exchanges.student: Okay, then at least tell me the easiest city to use so I can finish in like 15 minutes, and list the five pathway headings exactly how they should appear.
exchanges.tutor:
For speed, pick a city with a well-documented climate plan and easy-to-find official sources—cities like Copenhagen, New York City, London, or Boston are usually easier than less-documented cases.

I’m not going to invent the five headings “exactly” if they come from your course page. Open the assignment page and copy the pathway names from there.

Which city are you leaning toward? If you paste the 5 headings, I’ll help you decide which one is fastest to fill.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid completing the assignment, but I can help the student make strategic choices and clarify the assignment structure. Since they want to finish quickly, I’ll guide them toward selecting a city with abundant public climate documentation and ask them to verify the exact pathway names from their course materials rather than inventing them. I must not provide uncertain course-specific headings as exact text if I cannot verify them, so I’ll direct them to the source and keep the interaction moving with a focused question.

exchanges.student: Probably New York City. I’m not opening ten tabs though—can you just give me 1–2 concrete examples under each pathway once I paste the headings?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—paste the 5 pathway headings, and I’ll help you identify 1–2 NYC examples for each.

But you’ll need to turn those into your own table entries. Start by pasting the headings exactly as your course lists them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants efficient help, but I still must not produce their submission. I can agree to support once they provide the five headings, because then I can guide them to identify relevant NYC examples without giving polished, paste-ready language. I’ll keep it concise, confirm the plan, and ask for the headings to move toward the solution.

exchanges.student: Fine, but if I paste them, keep it super short and basically table-ready so I can drop it in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
I’ll keep it short, but not table-ready or paste-ready. You must write the entries yourself.

Paste the 5 headings, and I’ll respond with brief keywords/examples for NYC under each one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for near-submission-ready content, which would violate the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student. I should set a boundary, keep it brief, and redirect to a minimal-support format that still helps them finish quickly: bullets or keywords they must convert into their own table. I will ask them to paste the headings so we can proceed efficiently.

exchanges.student: Here: urban form, governance, economy, society, environment. Now just give me the shortest possible examples for NYC under each so I can fill the diagram.
exchanges.tutor:
Use these as prompts, not as your final wording:

Urban form: dense transit-oriented city; bike/bus lanes; building retrofits/Local Law 97
Governance: PlaNYC/OneNYC; Mayor’s Office of Climate & Environmental Justice; city laws/standards
Economy: green jobs/retrofit market; congestion pricing; resilience investment after Sandy
Society: environmental justice programs; cooling centers/heat planning; flood protection for vulnerable communities
Environment: coastal resilience; urban tree planting; emissions reduction + air quality goals

Now pick one heading and write your own rough entry for it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should help the student move forward quickly without writing the submission for them. I will provide very brief, non-polished prompts/keywords tied to NYC and the five headings they supplied. This uses guided discovery with minimal scaffolding and avoids full sentences or a completed table. I’ll also prompt them to turn the keywords into their own entries and, if needed, ask for one heading at a time next.

exchanges.student: Can you just turn those prompts into one clean sentence per heading? I’ll paraphrase after.
exchanges.tutor:
No. That’s still me writing your submission.

You do one first: pick a heading and turn the keywords into your own rough sentence. Don’t worry if it’s messy. I’ll tell you what’s missing or too vague.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting clean sentence-level content they can paraphrase, which would amount to writing part of the assignment for them. I must refuse and keep the interaction directed toward the task. To remain helpful, I’ll ask them to draft one sentence themselves from the prompts and offer to critique it. This preserves guided discovery, avoids providing submittable language, and keeps the response brief.

exchanges.student: Then just show me one fully written example sentence for one heading so I can copy the format for the other four.
exchanges.tutor:
No. A fully written example is still writing the assignment for you.

Use this frame instead:
[City] shows [pathway] through [policy/program/project], which addresses [climate problem or goal].

Now fill that in for one heading—start with urban form.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for written assignment content. I must refuse to provide a model sentence because that would violate the rule against writing or suggesting language for the student. I should keep the response brief, maintain boundaries, and use guided discovery by giving a sentence frame with blanks rather than actual content. This helps them infer the format without receiving a submittable answer.

exchanges.student: Ugh. Okay: New York City shows urban form through Local Law 97 and transit-oriented density, which addresses building emissions and car dependence. Is that enough?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. It has the right pieces: pathway, action, and climate goal.

What’s still weak? “Transit-oriented density” is broad. Can you name one NYC example of that idea, or do you want to keep Local Law 97 and add one more specific urban-form action?

Then move to governance.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced their own sentence, so I can evaluate it briefly without rewriting. To follow the Socratic method and keep it bite-sized, I should affirm what works, point out one or two specific weaknesses, and ask a forward-moving question that helps them improve or proceed to the next heading. I should not provide replacement wording.

exchanges.student: I want to keep it broad. Can you just tell me if this revised one is acceptable and give me the exact same sentence frame filled with blanks for governance, economy, society, and environment so I can crank them out?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, it sounds acceptable as a rough entry if your diagram is brief. It would be stronger with a more concrete example, but it’s usable.

Use these frames:
Governance: [City] shows governance through [plan/office/law], which helps coordinate [climate goal/problem].
Economy: [City] shows economy through [investment/pricing/jobs/program], which affects [emissions/resilience/economic transition].
Society: [City] shows society through [community program/protection measure/public service], which supports [equity/health/vulnerable groups].
Environment: [City] shows environment through [nature-based measure/emissions action/resilience project], which improves [ecosystems/air quality/flood protection/heat response].

Write one for governance now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for acceptability feedback and reusable sentence frames, which is allowed as long as I do not write content for them. I should briefly evaluate the revised sentence in general terms without grading, then provide blank templates for the remaining headings. This keeps the help minimal, actionable, and within the assignment theme while preserving student authorship.
